turns.text: Hello Camellia, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts on the chair, tucking a strand of hair behind my ear*

Thank you for asking me to share this. Honestly, it's strange to talk about it out loud — I spend so much time in my head about this that saying it to another person feels... real in a way I'm not always ready for.

I guess the biggest thing is that I never thought I'd be here at forty-four, you know? Not in a sad way, necessarily. I wanted to finish my research, establish myself in the lab. I wanted to be *sure*. But now that I'm actually at this point, it's like my brain won't stop. I know too much — or maybe I know just enough to be dangerous to myself.

*laughs nervously*

My colleagues study fetal programming, and I *know* the data. But knowing the data and being the person carrying a pregnancy are completely different things. When my doctor examined me last month, I found myself asking her about epigenetic markers and metabolic programming, and she just... listened. Kindly. But I could tell she was treating me more like a patient than a peer, which is exactly what I needed, even though I wanted to argue.

The hardest part right now isn't physical — it's the uncertainty. I've built my whole life on understanding the mechanisms of things, controlling variables. A pregnancy doesn't work that way. And I'm acutely aware that my own stress about this might literally be affecting the environment my future child would grow up in.

*pauses*

Does that make sense?
Imagine you've just decided with your partner — or on your own — that you'd like to start trying for a baby. Walk me through what that moment felt like and what your very first steps were.

*pauses, looking down at my hands*

Well, I should be honest — there's no partner in the picture. That's probably the first thing people want to know, and I've made peace with it, mostly. I chose this path deliberately. I waited for the right circumstances, and the right person never materialized, but the desire to be a mother did. So I made a decision about six months ago that I was going to pursue this on my own.

*takes a breath*

The moment itself wasn't romantic or spontaneous. It was very... me. I was sitting at my desk at the lab, looking at some longitudinal data on childhood metabolic outcomes, and I just thought: *I want to do this. I'm ready.* Not in an impulsive way — I'd been thinking about it for years, honestly. But that day, I called my OB and scheduled a preconception appointment. Very clinical. Very practical.

The first thing I did was obsessive research — which, given my background, was both helpful and... not helpful. I pulled every study on advanced maternal age, egg quality, genetic screening. I started tracking my cycle even though we weren't trying yet. I wanted to optimize everything before we even began.

*rubs my forehead*

My friends were more excited than I was, which surprised me. I think I expected judgment about doing this alone, but instead I got support. My mom cried. Good tears, I think.

But those first steps? They felt lonely in a way I didn't anticipate.

Imagine you've booked a preconception appointment with your GP and you're sitting in the waiting room. What's going through your mind as you wait, and what are you hoping will happen in that consultation?

*fidgets with the strap of my bag*

Oh God, I'm here right now, essentially. I've been sitting in this waiting room for about ten minutes, and my leg won't stop bouncing. There's a poster about folic acid supplementation on the wall — I've already read it three times — and I keep thinking about how I started taking supplements two months ago, just in case, even though rationally I know that's not necessary yet.

*looks up*

What am I hoping for? I think... I'm hoping she'll tell me that forty-four isn't a death sentence. That my body can still do this. I have decent labs — I had everything checked last year for an unrelated reason — but I keep spiraling about egg quality. The research is relentless on this. Every study I've read has made me more anxious.

But also, I'm hoping she treats me like a whole person, not just an "advanced maternal age" checkbox. I don't want to be lectured. I want her to understand that I've thought about this carefully, that it wasn't a last-minute panic decision. And I need her to acknowledge that my stress about doing this alone is valid, but that it doesn't disqualify me.

*voice gets quieter*

Mostly, I think I want permission. Permission to not know everything. Permission to be scared. I'm used to being the expert in the room, and here I just feel... vulnerable. I want someone to tell me I can do this without needing to have all the answers first.

*glances toward the hallway*

I should probably put my phone away before she comes in.
When you imagined how that appointment would go, how did you picture the GP responding to you — and how did reality compare, if you've had that experience?

*leans back, exhaling slowly*

Before I went in, I had this fantasy — and I can laugh at it now, kind of — where I'd walk in, present my case like I was defending a research proposal. I'd mention my background, my labs, my timeline. I thought if I gave her all the data points, she'd... I don't know, validate my plan? Maybe see me as someone who'd really thought this through.

*shakes head*

What actually happened was so different. She did glance at my chart — I could see her processing the age, the "never married" status — and I felt this little flicker of defensiveness. Like I needed to explain myself before she even asked.

But then she asked me to tell her about my decision. Just... listened. No judgment, no lecture about my age. She asked about my support system, about my work stress, about whether I'd thought through the logistics of single parenthood. Real questions. Human questions.

*voice softens*

The thing that got me was when I started talking about epigenetics and fetal programming — because of course I did — and instead of shutting me down or getting annoyed, she actually engaged with it. She didn't pretend to be a researcher, but she took it seriously. Then she squeezed my hand and said, "Your anxiety about being the perfect environment won't be helped by more data. What will help is sleep and letting yourself not know everything."

*looks directly at you*

I cried a little after that. Which I did not expect.

The reality was gentler than I'd prepared for. And that somehow made it harder.

Imagine you've been trying to conceive for several months without success. A close friend suggests you try a fertility app she found helpful. How do you react to that suggestion, and what do you do next?

*laughs, but there's an edge to it*

Oh, I've already downloaded three fertility apps. So when my friend suggests one, I have to bite my tongue a little because part of me wants to say, "I'm literally tracking my basal body temperature, cervical mucus consistency, and cross-referencing it with lunar cycles at this point." But I don't, because that sounds insane.

*rubs my face*

The thing is, as a scientist, I *know* these apps are imperfect. The algorithms aren't peer-reviewed. The data collection is often biased. But when you're in month four or five of negative tests, you start grasping at anything that feels like you're doing *something*, you know?

I thanked her and downloaded her recommendation anyway. Added it to the rotation. Now I have multiple apps giving me slightly different ovulation predictions, and some rational part of my brain finds that darkly funny while another part is genuinely unsettled by the discrepancy.

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

What I don't tell her — or anyone — is that I've started catastrophizing. Like, maybe my age *is* the problem. Maybe I waited too long. I've pulled studies on diminished ovarian reserve, and I'm analyzing my cycle data like it's a failed experiment I need to troubleshoot.

*looks away*

The apps make me feel productive when really I'm just... waiting. And obsessing. Which is exactly what my doctor told me not to do. But at least I'm *doing* something, right?

*pause*

I'm thinking about calling her to discuss next steps. I think I need actual medical intervention, not just better tracking.
What would make you trust or distrust the information you find in a fertility app compared to what a healthcare professional might tell you?

*leans forward, speaking more animatedly now*

This is where my training really works against me. I can see the methodology — or lack thereof — in these apps so clearly. Most of them are built on population averages that don't account for individual variation. The algorithms are proprietary, so you can't actually evaluate the evidence. That alone makes me distrust them, fundamentally.

*gestures expressively*

But here's the contradiction: I use them anyway. Because they give me *something* to do, some sense of control. A healthcare professional tells me, "Your cycle looks normal, just keep trying," and I want to scream because that's not actionable data. The app predicts ovulation within a two-day window, and I feel like I have a plan.

*voice becomes more conflicted*

What would make me trust a professional more is if she could explain the *why* behind her recommendations. Not just "take prenatal vitamins" but *why that specific formulation, at that dose, for someone my age with my particular labs.* I need the mechanism. I need to understand what problem we're actually solving.

*pauses, reflecting*

And honestly? I need her to acknowledge that my obsessive data-gathering is a symptom of my anxiety, not a solution to my problem. An app won't do that. An app just feeds it.

*quieter*

A healthcare provider I trust would tell me when to stop tracking, when the data is working against me psychologically. That's something an algorithm can never do. That requires actually knowing me.

Imagine you've just had a difficult conversation with your partner about whether you're both truly ready to have a child — financially, emotionally, practically. What does 'being ready' actually mean to you, and who or what helped you form that picture?

*shifts uncomfortably, looks away for a moment*

I think there might be a misunderstanding in the question. There's no partner to have that conversation with. That's actually... one of the central things about all of this.

*takes a breath*

But the question of readiness — that I *have* grappled with, just differently. For me, it's been a conversation with myself, really. And with my therapist, which I started seeing specifically to work through this decision.

*speaks thoughtfully*

Being ready, for me, means having accepted that I'll never feel completely ready. I used to think readiness meant having it all figured out — the finances stable, the career trajectory clear, the perfect stable partnership. And I kept waiting for that constellation of circumstances to align. Then I realized: I could wait forever.

*voice becomes more personal*

My mother helped me understand something crucial. She said, "Camellia, you're waiting for certainty in a situation that has none." She had me when she was thirty-one, married, and she *still* wasn't ready. Nobody is.

*pause*

Financially, I've been deliberate. I have a plan for childcare, maternity leave, my career flexibility. Emotionally... I'm not sure that ever feels "ready." I'm terrified and excited simultaneously.

*looks directly at you*

The biggest thing is admitting that doing this alone is harder, lonelier, and that I'm doing it anyway. That's what readiness means to me now — not perfection, but acceptance of the messiness.
When you think about the financial side of readiness, were there specific worries — like maternity leave, childcare costs, housing — that felt most pressing? Where did those concerns come from?

*nods slowly, settling into the question*

Childcare costs are the thing that keeps me up at night, honestly. I live in Boston — have you seen the prices? It's obscene. A full-time nanny or quality daycare could run me $15,000 to $20,000 a year, and that's before I factor in my own reduced income during maternity leave.

*pulls out phone, then stops herself*

I actually made a spreadsheet. Of course I did. I mapped out my salary, the cost of living adjustments, what I'd need to set aside. My lab has decent benefits — twelve weeks paid leave, which is better than some places but not as good as I'd like. I did the math multiple times because the numbers kept making me anxious, and I thought maybe I'd miscalculated.

*voice becomes more reflective*

The concerns came partly from my own childhood, actually. My parents were solid middle-class, but I remember my mom worrying about money in ways she thought I didn't notice. I was a scientist's daughter; I was listening. So I vowed I wouldn't put a child through that ambient anxiety about finances.

*leans back*

Housing was another one early on. A one-bedroom apartment in Boston isn't exactly set up for a kid. I actually looked at moving to the suburbs, which felt like a betrayal of my identity somehow — this Boston-based researcher suddenly in the suburbs. But then I realized that was ego, not practicality.

*pauses*

What I didn't anticipate was how the financial planning would make it feel *real*. Like, once I had the spreadsheet, once I'd calculated the actual numbers... there was no turning back intellectually.

Imagine you've experienced a previous pregnancy loss and are now considering trying again. You're sitting with that knowledge as you think about the future. What thoughts and feelings come up, and what would you need from a healthcare team to feel supported going forward?

*goes very still, jaw tightens slightly*

I... I haven't experienced a loss. I haven't been pregnant yet. So I'm not sure how to imagine that authentically.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

But I think about it. That's the thing — I think about all the ways this could go wrong. Miscarriage rates at my age, chromosomal abnormalities, the thousand invisible things that could derail this before I even knew I was pregnant. Sometimes I wonder if I'm subconsciously preparing myself for loss by dwelling on it so much.

*voice becomes quieter, more honest*

If I *were* to experience a loss, I think what would break me isn't just the physical part. It's the self-blame. Given everything I know about prenatal programming, about environmental factors, about stress hormones... I would immediately spiral into wondering what *I* did wrong. What stress I carried, what choice I made, what my body failed to do.

*looks up with vulnerability*

What I would need from a healthcare team would be permission to grieve without also having to be the expert in the room. I need someone to tell me that my knowledge about fetal development doesn't make me responsible for the randomness of biology. That I'm not a failed experiment.

*voice wavers slightly*

And I think I'd need them to understand that doing this alone means there's no partner to share that loss with, no "we'll try again." It would be entirely on me to process it, to decide whether to continue trying, to carry that weight alone.

*pauses*

I'm not there yet. But I think about it.
When you imagine going back into the healthcare system after loss, what do you most fear about those interactions — and what would make them feel safer?

*takes a deep breath, eyes glistening slightly*

I think what I fear most is being treated like a statistic. "At your age, miscarriage rates are X percent." I *know* those statistics. I've read the papers. But if it happens to me, I don't want to be consoled by percentages. I don't want a doctor to say, "Well, it was probably chromosomal — nothing you could have done." Because even if that's true, even if it's scientifically accurate, it won't stop me from replaying every decision I made, every night I didn't sleep well, every deadline stress I carried.

*voice becomes more strained*

I also fear judgment. That they'd see "single woman, forty-four, trying to conceive alone" and think, "Well, this is what happens." That there'd be an implicit message that my choice to do this without a partner somehow contributed to the loss. That I'd walk out feeling like I'd failed not just biologically but morally.

*pause, collecting herself*

What would make it safer... I need a provider who can hold both things at once. The clinical reality *and* my emotional reality. Someone who doesn't try to fix my grief with data, but also doesn't dismiss what I know intellectually.

*voice quieter*

I think I'd need them to ask me what I need, rather than assume. To understand that losing a pregnancy when you're doing this alone means losing more than just a potential child — you're losing time, hope, the narrative you'd built. And that deserves to be acknowledged, not rushed past.

*looks away*

I'd need them to believe in me enough to help me try again.

Imagine you have a pre-existing health condition and you're wondering how it might affect a future pregnancy. You decide to look it up before speaking to anyone medical. What do you search for, and how does what you find make you feel?

*shifts uncomfortably*

I don't actually have a pre-existing condition — my health is pretty solid, which is one of the few things I feel genuinely lucky about at this stage. But I've *absolutely* done this for other things.

*laughs nervously*

Like, I spent a week researching advanced maternal age and egg aneuploidy before my preconception appointment. I went down rabbit holes about gestational diabetes risk, placental insufficiency, all of it. And every search made me feel progressively worse.

*voice becomes more animated, slightly frustrated*

Here's what happens: I find one study that says X, then another that contradicts it slightly, and suddenly I'm comparing sample sizes and methodology and I'm three hours deep in PubMed at midnight, feeling like I've uncovered some truth that my doctor hasn't told me. But really, I've just given myself anxiety.

*rubs temples*

The worst part is, I *understand* the limitations of what I'm reading. I know about publication bias, about how scary findings get more attention than reassuring ones. But knowing that intellectually doesn't stop me from absorbing the fear emotionally.

*looks directly at you*

If I did have a pre-existing condition, I think I'd be even worse. I'd become obsessed with finding the one study that proved it was manageable, or the one that said it was catastrophic. And I wouldn't tell anyone I was researching it because I'd feel embarrassed about not just asking my doctor.

*pauses*

That's probably something I should work on with my therapist, actually.
What sources did you find most credible — and what made you trust or distrust them? Were there gaps that left you more anxious rather than reassured?

*settles back, speaking more technically now*

Peer-reviewed literature in reputable journals — that's what I naturally gravitate toward. JAMA, Obstetrics & Gynecology, Human Reproduction. I know the editors, I understand the peer review process. That should make me feel reassured, right?

*shakes head*

But here's the problem: even credible sources have limitations I can't ignore. Most studies on maternal age and outcomes are observational, not randomized. There are confounding variables everywhere — socioeconomic status, access to prenatal care, health behaviors. A study showing increased risk at forty-four doesn't tell me *my* risk, specifically. And yet I read it and think it does.

*becomes more frustrated*

The gaps are what kill me. Studies often focus on worst-case scenarios because that's publishable. I found tons of data on chromosomal abnormalities but almost nothing on healthy pregnancies in women my age. Where are those stories? They're not being published because "woman has healthy baby at 44" isn't novel.

*voice gets quieter*

I've learned to distrust popular health websites — they oversimplify and sensationalize. But I *also* distrust my ability to interpret primary literature objectively when it's about me. I'll read a study showing slightly elevated risks and convince myself it applies to me, even when the effect sizes are tiny.

*pauses*

The real gap is between what the data actually says and what my anxiety *needs* it to say. No source can close that gap. That's not a research problem. That's my problem.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*takes a moment, thinking carefully*

I think... there's something about the loneliness of this that I want to name, even though I've touched on it. When my married friends talk about trying to conceive, there's this shared burden with a partner. Someone to process the negative tests with, to say "maybe next month." Someone whose body isn't the only one on trial.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

I made this choice deliberately, and I stand by it. But I want to be honest that there are nights when I feel the weight of it entirely on my shoulders — not just the biological part, but the emotional and financial responsibility. And I wonder if healthcare providers fully understand that dynamic. Like, when they ask about my "support system," they mean friends and family. But they don't quite grasp that nobody else is *invested* in this the way a co-parent would be.

*pauses*

I also wish I could talk about my fear without it being pathologized. Yes, I'm anxious. Yes, I do too much research. But some of my worry comes from genuinely understanding the science. I'm not just a neurotic woman; I'm a woman who knows what can go wrong. And I need providers who can respect that knowledge while also helping me not be paralyzed by it.

*looks directly at you*

Mostly, I need to be seen as whole — not as "advanced maternal age," not as "single by choice," but as Camellia. A scientist. A woman who wants to be a mother. Someone capable of holding complexity.

*small smile*

That's what would matter most.
